full_response:
Let's carefully answer the matrix multiplication question step by step.

### Problem restatement

Given two square \(3\times 3\) matrices \([M]\) and \([N]\) with entries \(M_{ij}\) and \(N_{ij}\) (with \(i,j = 1,2,3\)), write the matrix product \([N][M]\) using the \(\Sigma\) (sum) notation.

---

## Step 1: Define the Product

If
\[
[N] =
\begin{bmatrix}
N_{11} & N_{12} & N_{13} \\
N_{21} & N_{22} & N_{23} \\
N_{31} & N_{32} & N_{33} \\
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
[M] =
\begin{bmatrix}
M_{11} & M_{12} & M_{13} \\
M_{21} & M_{22} & M_{23} \\
M_{31} & M_{32} & M_{33} \\
\end{bmatrix}
\]
Then the \( (i,j) \) entry of the product matrix \([N][M]\) is given by:

\[
([N][M])_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^3 N_{ik} M_{kj}
\]

where:

- \(i\) is the row of \([N]\) (and of the product)
- \(j\) is the column of \([M]\) (and of the product)
- \(k\) is summed over (matrix multiplication contracts over the inner dimension).

---

## Step 2: Explicit Summation Notation

So,
\[
([N][M])_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^3 N_{ik} M_{kj}
\]
This applies for all entries \(i, j = 1,2,3\).

---

## Step 3: Box the Answer

\[
\boxed{
([N][M])_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^3 N_{ik} M_{kj}
}
\]

---

Confidence: 100%
